title: RMSprop: Fixing AdaGrad's Decay Problem
concept: AdaGrad's learning rates shrink so much over time that training can grind to a halt; RMSprop fixes this by using a moving average of RECENT gradients instead of accumulating every past gradient forever.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what's the core idea behind AdaGrad's per-parameter learning rates?

options.text: It accumulates a running total of each parameter's past squared gradients, and shrinks that parameter's rate the more it's been updated
It gives every parameter a completely random learning rate
It only adjusts the learning rate for the output layer

exercises.explain: Right — AdaGrad shrinks each parameter's rate based on its own accumulated squared gradients. But that accumulation NEVER stops growing. What happens after a very long training run?

cards.title: When learning grinds to a halt
cards.body: Imagine training a huge network with AdaGrad for a very long time. Early progress is great — rare weights get big, useful steps. But partway through training, weight updates start barely moving at all anymore, even though the model clearly hasn't finished learning. What went wrong?

cards.title: RMSprop: a moving average instead of a running total
cards.body: RMSprop (Root Mean Square Propagation) tracks each parameter's squared gradients using a moving average that only remembers RECENT gradients, letting old ones gradually fade out — instead of AdaGrad's running total that adds up everything since the very beginning and never forgets.

cards.title: A 7-day forecast, not a year-to-date total
cards.body: AdaGrad is like tracking total rainfall for the entire year — the number only ever climbs, and one soggy January can make every dry summer month look irrelevant. RMSprop is like a 7-day weather average: it reflects RECENT conditions, so it stays useful no matter how long the year has gone on.

cards.title: Comparing training curves over time
cards.body: Watch step sizes over thousands of training steps: with AdaGrad, step sizes keep shrinking and shrinking until they're practically zero — training stalls. With RMSprop, step sizes level off at a usefully sized value and stay there, because old gradients keep fading out of its moving average instead of piling up forever.

cards.title: RMSprop still gives per-parameter rates — it just doesn't freeze
cards.body: It's easy to assume RMSprop throws out AdaGrad's whole idea. It doesn't — RMSprop still gives every parameter its own individually scaled learning rate, exactly like AdaGrad. The ONLY change is swapping AdaGrad's ever-growing sum for a decaying moving average, so the learning rate never permanently freezes.

left.text:
Accumulates ALL past squared gradients forever
Learning rate can shrink to near-zero

right.text:
Moving average of RECENT squared gradients
Learning rate stays usefully sized

exercises.prompt: What is the key difference between how AdaGrad and RMSprop track past gradients?

options.text: RMSprop uses a decaying moving average of recent squared gradients instead of accumulating all of them forever
RMSprop ignores gradients entirely and uses random steps
RMSprop only tracks the very first gradient of training

exercises.explain: Correct! That decaying average is exactly what keeps RMSprop's learning rates from shrinking to nothing.

exercises.prompt: True or false: RMSprop's per-parameter learning rate can eventually grind to a near-halt the same way AdaGrad's can, because it also accumulates every gradient since training began.
exercises.explain: False. RMSprop's moving average lets old gradients fade out, so its learning rates stay usable through long training runs instead of decaying to near-zero.

exercises.sentence: RMSprop replaces AdaGrad's ever-growing sum of past gradients with a ___ average that focuses on recent gradients.
exercises.explain: A moving average keeps only recent gradients relevant, letting old ones fade instead of piling up forever.

exercises.prompt: Your team trains a very deep network for thousands of steps. With AdaGrad, updates nearly stop midway through training. With RMSprop, updates keep making steady progress the whole time. Which property explains the difference?

options.text: RMSprop's decaying moving average of recent gradients, instead of an ever-growing accumulation
RMSprop uses a completely different loss function

exercises.explain: Exactly — RMSprop's moving average is the fix for AdaGrad's forever-shrinking learning rate problem.

exercises.prompt: Your team trains a large network for 50,000 steps using AdaGrad. Halfway through, weight updates nearly stop entirely, even though validation loss is clearly still improving slowly. Which change addresses the root cause?

options.text: Switch to RMSprop, which uses a decaying moving average of recent squared gradients instead of an ever-growing sum, so learning rates don't shrink to near-zero
Add more Momentum on top of AdaGrad
Switch from ReLU to Sigmoid activations

exercises.explain: Exactly right! RMSprop fixed AdaGrad's decay problem — but it still doesn't have Momentum's velocity for smoothing zig-zags. Next up: Adam, which combines BOTH ideas into one optimizer.
